quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Urban sprawl"?

options.text: Low-density, geographically expansive development pattern characterized by sprawling neighborhoods, shopping centers, and dispersed employment
Average number of children per woman; replacement level is ~2.1 in developed countries, <2.0 in many developed countries
The physical removal of soil by water, wind, or ice, separating and transporting particles away from the original soil location
Loss of core cooling resulting in extreme temperatures, potential fuel melting, and radioactive release

quiz_questions.explanation: "Urban sprawl" is defined as: Low-density, geographically expansive development pattern characterized by sprawling neighborhoods, shopping centers, and dispersed employment. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Brownfield"?

options.text: Reproductive failure where eggshells become fragile and break during incubation, caused by DDE (DDT metabolite) disrupting calcium metabolism
Previously developed property potentially contaminated by hazardous waste, suitable for redevelopment within urban areas
A large geographic area with characteristic climate, vegetation, and animal communities adapted to specific environmental conditions
A population maintaining relatively constant size around carrying capacity through density-dependent regulation

quiz_questions.explanation: "Brownfield" is defined as: Previously developed property potentially contaminated by hazardous waste, suitable for redevelopment within urban areas. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Greenfield"?

options.text: Undeveloped land, typically agricultural or natural, developed for urban expansion
Total greenhouse gas emissions from extracting, processing, transporting, and burning a fuel
The pathway of a chemical element or nutrient through the biotic and abiotic components of an ecosystem
A boundary between air masses with different temperature and humidity, driving precipitation and weather changes

quiz_questions.explanation: "Greenfield" is defined as: Undeveloped land, typically agricultural or natural, developed for urban expansion. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Transit-oriented development (TOD)"?

options.text: Population increase at a constant rate per capita, producing J-shaped growth curve when resources are unlimited
Atmospheric gas (CO₂, CH₄, N₂O, fluorine gases) absorbing infrared radiation and warming earth's surface
High-density residential and mixed-use development within walking distance of transit (bus, rail)
Numerical scale converting pollutant concentrations to public health categories (good, moderate, unhealthy, hazardous)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Transit-oriented development (TOD)" is defined as: High-density residential and mixed-use development within walking distance of transit (bus, rail). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Urban heat island"?

options.text: Scarcity of required resources (food, water, nesting sites) preventing population increase and causing intraspecific competition
Continued population growth despite replacement fertility, from age structure weighted toward reproductive years
Systemic placement of hazardous facilities (landfills, refineries, incinerators) in communities of color and low-income areas
Phenomenon where cities experience higher temperatures than surrounding rural areas from reduced vegetation and heat-absorbing pavement

quiz_questions.explanation: "Urban heat island" is defined as: Phenomenon where cities experience higher temperatures than surrounding rural areas from reduced vegetation and heat-absorbing pavement. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Low-density, geographically expansive development pattern characterized by sprawling neighborhoods, shopping centers, and dispersed employment"?

options.text: Mass extinction
Genetic diversity
Climate justice
Urban sprawl

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Urban sprawl".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Previously developed property potentially contaminated by hazardous waste, suitable for redevelopment within urban areas"?

options.text: Ventilation rate
Population doubling time
Brownfield
Tipping point

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Brownfield".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Undeveloped land, typically agricultural or natural, developed for urban expansion"?

options.text: Cation exchange capacity (CEC)
Convection
Hydroelectric power
Greenfield

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Greenfield".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "High-density residential and mixed-use development within walking distance of transit (bus, rail)"?

options.text: Drought
Genetic diversity
Habitat corridor
Transit-oriented development (TOD)

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Transit-oriented development (TOD)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Phenomenon where cities experience higher temperatures than surrounding rural areas from reduced vegetation and heat-absorbing pavement"?

options.text: Halons
Restoration intensity
Hydroelectric power
Urban heat island

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Urban heat island".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Urbanization and Sprawl is accurate?

options.text: Waste composition varies regionally but overwhelmingly includes paper (35%), plastics (12%), food (8%), and metals (8%), with most waste ending in landfills.

Urbanization and Sprawl describe the concentration of population in cities (now 55% of humanity) and low-density expansion into surrounding land consuming agricultural land, fragmenting habitats, increasing infrastructure costs, and generating...

Soil Fertility and Erosion describe soil's capacity to provide available nutrients for plant growth and the detachment and transport of soil by water and wind, which threatens global food security by removing productive topsoil accumulated over...
Population Age Structure describes the distribution of individuals across age classes, and fundamentally determines population growth rates, migration potential, and demographic projections.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Urbanization and Sprawl as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Urbanization and Sprawl?

options.text: Mining and Land Degradation involve extraction of mineral and energy resources essential for modern society while causing severe environmental degradation including water pollution, habitat destruction, acid drainage, and waste accumulation...
Effective conservation requires understanding complex population interactions and managing multiple threatening processes simultaneously.
Sprawling metropolitan areas have lower density than compact cities, require longer commute distances, and generate greater per capita emissions despite sometimes lower overall carbon intensity.

Ecosystem Services and Biomes provide tangible and intangible benefits to humans, including provisioning services (food, water, materials), regulating services (climate regulation, disease control), supporting services (nutrient cycling,...

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Urbanization and Sprawl as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Urbanization and Sprawl?

options.text: Human Population Growth has accelerated from 1 billion (1800) to 8 billion (2023), driven primarily by mortality reduction while fertility rates lag in developing countries, creating a population momentum continuing growth to 9–10 billion by...

Resource Depletion describes how humanity is depleting renewable and non-renewable resources faster than they are replenished or regenerated, including fossil fuels, topsoil, freshwater aquifers, fisheries, and forests, creating scarcity and...
Sustainable urbanization requires compact development, transit-oriented growth, and agricultural protection through greenbelts and development boundaries.

Deforestation occurs through logging, agricultural expansion (especially cattle ranching and soy cultivation), infrastructure development, and human settlement, occurring at rates of approximately 10 million hectares annually.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Urbanization and Sprawl as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Urbanization and Sprawl?

options.text: Agricultural Practices and Impacts shape more than 40% of earth's land surface, fundamentally altering soil, water, biodiversity, and biogeochemical cycles through cultivation, fertilizer application, pesticide use, and irrigation.
Successful restoration requires understanding the original ecosystem's composition, structure, and ecological processes, then removing limiting factors and facilitating species reestablishment.
Energy efficiency improvements are constrained by rebound effects where lower energy costs increase demand, partially offsetting efficiency gains.

Urbanization and Sprawl describe the concentration of population in cities (now 55% of humanity) and low-density expansion into surrounding land consuming agricultural land, fragmenting habitats, increasing infrastructure costs, and generating...

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Urbanization and Sprawl as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Urbanization and Sprawl?

options.text: Poor indoor air quality contributes to respiratory disease, asthma, allergies, and sick building syndrome, creating significant health and economic costs.
Population Ecology studies the dynamics of groups of interbreeding individuals of the same species, examining how populations grow, decline, and interact with their environment and other species.
Sprawling metropolitan areas have lower density than compact cities, require longer commute distances, and generate greater per capita emissions despite sometimes lower overall carbon intensity.
Understanding weather patterns is essential for agriculture, disaster preparedness, and predicting climate change impacts.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Urbanization and Sprawl as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Urbanization and Sprawl?

options.text: Implementing urban growth boundaries (Portland, Oregon) to contain sprawl and preserve agricultural land while increasing core city density
Treating municipal wastewater through primary (settling), secondary (biological), and tertiary (nutrient removal) steps to protect receiving waters
Restoring mined lands by deliberately introducing pioneer species and managing succession toward productive ecosystems or wildlife habitat
Creating wildlife corridors such as the Path of the Panther in Florida to connect fragmented habitats and enable panther population recovery

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Urbanization and Sprawl.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Urbanization and Sprawl?

options.text: Predicting erosion vulnerability by mapping soil properties and slope to identify areas requiring conservation practices
Reducing power plant SO₂ emissions through fuel switching to low-sulfur coal, coal desulfurization, or renewable energy transition
Developing bus rapid transit (BRT) systems providing rapid, affordable urban transportation reducing automobile dependence and emissions
Analyzing how wolf predation limits elk population growth in Yellowstone, affecting vegetation recovery and demonstrating top-down control

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Urbanization and Sprawl.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Urbanization and Sprawl applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Managing harmful algal blooms (cyanobacteria) in freshwater lakes through nutrient reduction and copper sulfate applications in emergency situations
Restoring mined lands by deliberately introducing pioneer species and managing succession toward productive ecosystems or wildlife habitat
Rehabilitating brownfield sites for mixed-use development (residential, commercial, recreational) reconnecting urban areas and increasing density
Monitoring topsoil loss in agricultural regions using aerial surveys to guide implementation of windbreaks, contour plowing, and buffers

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Urbanization and Sprawl.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Urbanization and Sprawl?

options.text: Using succession principles in invasive species management by understanding how native species can reestablish and outcompete invasives
Decommissioning aging nuclear plants through radioactive material removal, facility decontamination, and site remediation
Restoring mountaintop removal mining sites in Appalachia through wildlife habitat creation and carbon sequestration on reclaimed land
Implementing urban growth boundaries (Portland, Oregon) to contain sprawl and preserve agricultural land while increasing core city density

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Urbanization and Sprawl.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Urbanization and Sprawl in practice?

options.text: Developing bus rapid transit (BRT) systems providing rapid, affordable urban transportation reducing automobile dependence and emissions
Supporting agroforestry systems integrating trees with crops to improve soil quality, increase biodiversity, and enhance farmer resilience
Projecting human population growth using demographic transition models to plan infrastructure, food production, and resource allocation
Monitoring BOD and dissolved oxygen in rivers to assess organic pollution severity and predict fish kills during low-flow periods

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Urbanization and Sprawl.
